questions.question: Explain the rules for naming identifiers in Python. Also differentiate between valid and invalid identifiers with examples. Can an identifier start with an underscore? Discuss the significance of leading, trailing, and double underscores in identifier naming.
keyPoints: Identifiers can contain letters, digits, and underscores
Cannot start with a digit
Case-sensitive
Cannot be a keyword
Single underscore _ is valid but has special meaning
Double underscore __ has special meaning in classes
Leading underscore indicates private/protected
Reserved words cannot be used as identifiers

questions.question: What are Python keywords? List at least 10 reserved keywords and explain why they cannot be used as identifiers. Also explain how you can check the list of keywords in Python programmatically.
keyPoints: Keywords are reserved words with special meaning
Cannot be used as identifiers or function names
Examples: if, else, for, while, class, def, import, return, True, False, None
Use keyword module to check keywords
Cannot be modified or redefined

questions.question: Explain different types of literals in Python with examples. How do string literals differ from numeric literals? What is the difference between integer literals in different number systems (binary, octal, hexadecimal)?
keyPoints: String literals: single, double, triple quotes
Numeric literals: integers, floats, complex numbers
Boolean literals: True, False
None literal
Special literals: None
Raw strings, f-strings, byte strings
Integer bases: 0b (binary), 0o (octal), 0x (hexadecimal)

questions.question: Write a Python program to demonstrate the use of different identifier naming conventions: camelCase, PascalCase, and snake_case. Also explain which convention Python recommends for different scenarios (variables, functions, classes, constants).
keyPoints: camelCase: first letter lowercase, subsequent words uppercase (e.g., myVariable)
PascalCase: each word starts with uppercase (e.g., MyClass)
snake_case: words separated by underscores (e.g., my_variable)
PEP 8 recommends snake_case for functions and variables
Constants should be UPPER_SNAKE_CASE
Classes should use PascalCase
Private identifiers start with single underscore
